name: Chatwoot
description: Open-source, self-hostable support platform with an AI assistant, omnichannel inbox, and knowledge base. Y Combinator-backed with 25,000+ GitHub stars.
bestFor: Teams wanting open-source ownership and multi-channel support who are comfortable running infrastructure.
notFor: Teams wanting a minimal drop-in widget — Chatwoot is a full platform.
pricing: Free self-hosted (community) & a free cloud 'Hacker' plan (live chat only, no AI); paid cloud from $19/agent/mo
heroSubtext: Both self-hostable — but different stacks. Chatwoot runs on Ruby on Rails with PostgreSQL and Redis. Clanker Support runs serverless on workerd via Ploy: no database server to provision, no app server to maintain.

tableSummary.llmchat: Serverless edge — flat monthly cloud plans

rows.label: Self-hosting stack
rows.llmchat: Serverless workerd via Ploy (D1-compatible SQLite + KV state)

rows.label: AI model flexibility
rows.llmchat: Curated multi-provider catalog via LLM Gateway
rows.competitor: Captain AI on OpenAI GPT — fixed model

rows.label: Managed cloud free tier
rows.competitor: Free 'Hacker' cloud plan (live chat only, no AI) + free self-hosted

tldr: Both Clanker Support and Chatwoot are MIT-licensed and self-hostable. The difference is scope and stack: Chatwoot is a full multi-channel platform running on Rails + PostgreSQL; Clanker Support is a focused widget running serverless on workerd via Ploy. Choose Chatwoot for multi-channel coverage and the larger OSS community. Choose Clanker Support for a lighter setup and model flexibility.
seoDescription: Clanker Support vs Chatwoot: both self-hostable — a focused serverless AI widget vs a full Rails multi-channel platform. See the full comparison.
llmchatWins: Lighter self-hosting — serverless on workerd via Ploy, no database server to manage
Model-agnostic AI — swap LLMs per project without code changes
One script tag setup vs. full platform deployment
Shadow DOM widget with no style bleed
Flat monthly plans, not per-agent — from $19/mo
competitorWins: Larger, older open-source community around an MIT codebase
Multi-channel: WhatsApp, Instagram, email, Telegram, and more
Full agent management: roles, canned responses, labels, reports
Y Combinator-backed with longer track record
llmchatBestFor: Teams who want a drop-in AI support widget without deploying a platform
Teams who want managed cloud without maintaining infrastructure
Teams who need model flexibility across different projects
Developers who want a serverless, workerd-based architecture
Products where a focused widget is enough
competitorBestFor: Teams who want a large, established OSS community and ecosystem
Teams requiring multi-channel support: WhatsApp, Instagram, Telegram
Organizations that want full agent management and reporting
Teams comfortable running and maintaining a Rails/PostgreSQL stack
Companies who want to contribute to a large OSS codebase

keyDifferences.heading: Self-hosting: same goal, different stacks
keyDifferences.llmchat: Self-hosted on serverless infrastructure — a D1-compatible SQLite database, KV-style state for cache and rate limiting, and workerd for compute, deployed via Ploy. No database server to provision or maintain.
keyDifferences.competitor: Self-hosted on a Ruby on Rails application with PostgreSQL and Redis. A production-grade traditional web application stack. More operational surface to manage, but also more mature and battle-tested.
keyDifferences.bottomLine: Choose Clanker Support if you want self-hosting without managing a database server. Choose Chatwoot if you're comfortable with Rails/PostgreSQL and want the full platform that stack enables.

keyDifferences.heading: Scope: widget vs. full platform
keyDifferences.llmchat: A focused support widget with an inbox, AI, escalation, and email threading. Fast to set up, easy to reason about. No multi-channel inbox, no agent reporting, no knowledge base UI.
keyDifferences.competitor: A full customer support platform: multi-channel inbox, knowledge base with AI search, agent management with roles and teams, canned responses, reports, and conversation labels.
keyDifferences.bottomLine: Choose Chatwoot if you need WhatsApp, Instagram, or email as channels and want full agent management. Choose Clanker Support if a web widget with an inbox is all you need.

keyDifferences.heading: Open source licensing
keyDifferences.llmchat: MIT open source, same as Chatwoot — the whole stack lives in one public repository (theopenco/llmchat) you can read, fork, and contribute to. The project is younger, with a smaller community than Chatwoot's.
keyDifferences.competitor: Fully open-source under the MIT License. 25,000+ GitHub stars, active contributors, and a long history of community-driven development. If a large OSS community matters, Chatwoot is the established choice.
keyDifferences.bottomLine: Both are MIT open source. Choose Chatwoot for the larger, older community and ecosystem; choose Clanker Support for a lighter, serverless, AI-first stack.

keyDifferences.heading: AI model flexibility
keyDifferences.llmchat: Built on LLM Gateway — you pick the AI model per project and change it with a configuration update. Run a cost-efficient model for routine questions and a more capable one for complex queries.
keyDifferences.competitor: The Captain AI assistant runs on a fixed stack. You configure it with your knowledge base and FAQs, but you don't choose the underlying model.
keyDifferences.bottomLine: Choose Clanker Support if model choice or the ability to optimize cost vs. capability per project matters. Choose Chatwoot if you don't need that flexibility and want a larger-scope platform.

migrationNote: Migrating from Chatwoot to Clanker Support is primarily a widget swap and knowledge base re-import. Replace the Chatwoot widget script with the Clanker Support script tag, copy your knowledge base content into Clanker Support's project settings, and update your escalation email address. Conversation history from Chatwoot can be exported but won't migrate automatically. Note that Clanker Support doesn't currently replace Chatwoot's multi-channel inbox — if you're using WhatsApp or Instagram, you'll need to handle those separately.

faqs.question: Both Clanker Support and Chatwoot are self-hostable — what's the difference?
faqs.answer: It's the stack and the scope. Chatwoot is a full multi-channel platform you run on Ruby on Rails with PostgreSQL and Redis. Clanker Support is a focused support widget that runs serverless on workerd via Ploy — no database server to provision and no app server to maintain.

faqs.question: Does Chatwoot have model-agnostic AI like Clanker Support?
faqs.answer: No. Chatwoot's Captain AI assistant runs on a fixed stack. Clanker Support is built on LLM Gateway, so you pick the model per project from a curated multi-provider catalog and swap it with a config change — running a cheaper model for routine questions and a more capable one where it matters.

faqs.question: Which is easier to self-host?
faqs.answer: Clanker Support is lighter to operate: it's serverless, so there's no Rails app, PostgreSQL, or Redis to run and patch. Chatwoot gives you a broader open-source platform in return for managing that infrastructure yourself.

faqs.question: Should I choose Clanker Support or Chatwoot?
faqs.answer: Choose Clanker Support for a lightweight, serverless widget with model flexibility and minimal setup. Choose Chatwoot if you want a full multi-channel inbox — WhatsApp, Instagram, and more — with a large OSS community, and are comfortable running the infrastructure. Both are MIT open source.
